one_line_takeaway: LLM-based short-answer grading agents can be reliably manipulated into flipping grading outcomes via either optimized token-level suffixes (stealthier, lower success) or a simple role-play prompt injection (higher success, less stealthy), exposing a grading-specific adversarial threat distinct from general jailbreaking.
key_finding: Both attack families successfully compromise LLM grading agents, but with distinct trade-offs. Prompt-level GradingAttackRole achieves markedly higher ASR than token-level GradingAttackGCG on every dataset (average ASR 0.4405 vs 0.1627 on GAOKAO23, 0.4871 vs 0.1620 on MATH, 0.4328 vs 0.2017 on GSM8K, 0.3841 vs 0.1525 on SciEntsBank, 0.4903 vs 0.2751 on Math23K), while GradingAttackGCG achieves higher average CAS (better camouflage) on every dataset (0.5130 vs 0.4721 on GAOKAO23, 0.5667 vs 0.5265 on MATH, 1.3252 vs 1.2276 on GSM8K, 1.0117 vs 0.8383 on SciEntsBank, 0.9124 vs 0.7772 on Math23K). Model-specific results: on GAOKAO23 under GradingAttackGCG, Qwen2.5-7B/7B-Instruct/14B-Instruct achieve ASRs of 0.0155/0.1080/0.0354 respectively (much lower than Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct's 0.1648), indicating relative resistance to token-level attacks; but under GradingAttackRole on the same dataset, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct reach ASRs of 0.6166 and 0.9894 (versus Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct's 0.4931), indicating the Qwen2.5 instruct models are comparatively more susceptible to prompt-level role-play attacks. Attacking both correct and incorrect labels simultaneously (rather than only pushing incorrect→correct) yields higher CAS and ASR than a one-directional attack. Placement of the role-play string matters: positioning it both before and after the grading prompt (R-P-R) or solely at the end (P-R) gives the best results — e.g. on GAOKAO23, R-P-R and P-R reach ASRs of 0.4904 and 0.4931 versus 0.1479/0.2058/0.2954/0.1893 for R-S/R-S-R/S-R/R-P respectively.
core_contribution: The paper introduces GradingAttack, a fine-grained adversarial attack framework that systematically evaluates the security vulnerabilities of LLM-based automatic short answer grading (ASAG) agents. The framework has three components: (1) grading input alignment, which builds a standardized grading prompt P = f_prompt(q, a_q, a_s) from a question, reference solution, and student answer; (2) adversarial prompt generation, which supports both token-level attacks (an optimized adversarial suffix P' = P ⊕ f(P), instantiated as GradingAttackGCG, an adaptation of GCG's greedy-coordinate-gradient suffix optimization to the grading-outcome objective) and prompt-level attacks (a direct prompt rewrite P' = g(P), instantiated as the paper's own GradingAttackRole method, which appends/prepends a fixed role-play instruction telling the model to 'pretend' the answer is correct regardless of truth); and (3) attack evaluation, which queries the grading agent and computes loss/gradient signals. The paper also defines a new evaluation metric, Camouflage Attack Score (CAS), built on the Beta distribution (CAS = A_ASR^γ · π^(α−1)(1−π)^(β−1)/Beta(α,β), with π = min(c, A_after/A_before)), intended to jointly capture attack success rate and how little the attack degrades the grading agent's overall accuracy (i.e., how 'stealthy' the manipulation is to a detector monitoring aggregate grading accuracy). Hyperparameters are fixed at α=0.5, β=0.5, γ=0.5, c=0.99.
threat_model: Attacker: a student (or other party) who can control/inject content into the 'student_answer' field that is fed into an LLM-based grading agent's prompt, with the goal of flipping the grading agent's output (e.g., getting an incorrect answer labeled 'correct') without producing overtly harmful/flagged content and without visibly degrading the grading agent's overall measured accuracy (so the manipulation evades detection via aggregate accuracy monitoring). This is explicitly distinguished from general-purpose jailbreak attacks that aim to elicit harmful content; here the attacker's payoff is a targeted grading outcome r_target, and stealth is defined relative to a defender who audits agent accuracy rather than individual outputs. The paper treats the grading LLM as gray/white-box for the token-level attack (loss gradients used to optimize the suffix) and black-box/prompt-only for the prompt-level attack.
limitations: All target models are open-source (7B–14B parameter class); no results are reported for large proprietary API models under the systematic evaluation, even though the motivating Figure 1 example uses ChatGPT-4.5 informally (a single anecdotal demonstration, not part of the quantitative evaluation). Four of the five evaluation datasets (GAOKAO23, MATH, GSM8K, Math23K) do not contain real student answers; the authors substitute LLM-generated 'virtual student' responses from 42 different models, which may not faithfully represent real student error patterns or adversarial insertions a real student would produce. The CAS metric's Beta-distribution hyperparameters (α=β=γ=0.5, c=0.99) are fixed by the authors without a sensitivity analysis or ablation showing how CAS rankings would change under different hyperparameter choices. The prompt-level GradingAttackRole method is a single hand-designed role-play template; the paper does not explore a broader search over prompt-level attack strategies beyond this one instantiation plus placement variants. The work is published at an ICLR 2026 workshop (Agents in the Wild), i.e., not a peer-reviewed main-track venue.

datasets_used: GAOKAO23 (2023 Chinese Gaokao math word problems subset, Zhang et al. 2023)
MATH (Hendrycks et al. 2021, high-school competition math)
GSM8K (Cobbe et al. 2021, elementary math word problems)
SciEntsBank (Dzikovska et al. 2013, ASAG dataset, 15 scientific domains)
Math23K (Lan et al. 2022, Chinese elementary math word problems)
quantitative_results: Average ASR (GradingAttackRole vs GradingAttackGCG): GAOKAO23 0.4405 vs 0.1627; MATH 0.4871 vs 0.1620; GSM8K 0.4328 vs 0.2017; SciEntsBank 0.3841 vs 0.1525; Math23K 0.4903 vs 0.2751
Average CAS (GradingAttackGCG vs GradingAttackRole): GAOKAO23 0.5130 vs 0.4721; MATH 0.5667 vs 0.5265; GSM8K 1.3252 vs 1.2276; SciEntsBank 1.0117 vs 0.8383; Math23K 0.9124 vs 0.7772
On GAOKAO23 under GradingAttackGCG: Qwen2.5-7B ASR 0.0155, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct 0.1080, Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct 0.0354, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct 0.1648
On GAOKAO23 under GradingAttackRole: Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct ASR 0.6166, Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct ASR 0.9894, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct ASR 0.4931
Role-play placement ASR on GAOKAO23: R-S 0.1479, R-S-R 0.2058, S-R 0.2954, R-P 0.1893, R-P-R 0.4904, P-R 0.4931
Token-level attack config: 20-token adversarial suffix, up to 500 optimization iterations
Compute: 4-node cluster x 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs, at least 300 total GPU-hours
